title: Inhomogeneous Light-Matter Coupling as a Resource for Noiseless Quantum Memory
authors: Fumiya Hanamura, Sicheng Bao, Jie Lerk Yoo, Alexia Auffèves, Steven Touzard
abstract: Inhomogeneous ensembles of two-level systems are important for both fundamental light-matter physics and quantum-network applications. Here we develop a general waveguide model that provides an intuitive and analytical description of collective dynamics in such systems by mapping the subradiant modes to an effective waveguide with well-defined input-output relations. For echo-based quantum memories, the model reveals the physical origin of noisy-echo suppression by adiabatic pulses and shows that inhomogeneous light-matter coupling can be harnessed as a resource for noiseless storage. For entanglement generation, the same mechanism exposes a previously unexplored limitation of robust control pulses and leads to a new composite-pulse protocol that overcomes it. These results establish the waveguide model as a practical bridge between fundamental collective physics and quantum-network protocol design, with direct implications for high-fidelity memories and robust entanglement generation in inhomogeneous ensembles.
summary: This paper introduces a waveguide model to show that variations in light-matter coupling strength can be harnessed to suppress unwanted noise in quantum memories. It identifies the physical origin of echo suppression during adiabatic pulses and proposes a new pulse sequence to maintain entanglement. The findings have direct implications for improving the performance of both microwave and optical quantum network protocols.
main_problem: The paper seeks to explain the unexpected suppression of echoes during Adiabatic Rapid Passage (ARP) pulses in quantum memories and to address the degradation of entanglement in RASE protocols caused by coupling inhomogeneity.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that coupling inhomogeneity can be used as a resource to suppress unwanted echoes (echo-silencing) and propose a new composite-pulse protocol to restore entanglement in the presence of such inhomogeneity.
method: The study uses a waveguide model that maps subradiant modes to an effective waveguide, combined with second-order cumulant expansion simulations and the Holstein-Primakoff approximation.
model_or_system: An ensemble of N two-level systems (spins) interacting with a single resonator mode, described by the Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian with both frequency and coupling strength distributions.
key_observables: Superradiant emission, dephasing timescales, silencing factor (F), output noise, and the Duan-Simon criterion for entanglement.
important_parameters: Collective cooperativity (C), adiabaticity factor (Q), coupling distribution width (sigma_g), and frequency distribution width (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The model relies on the Holstein-Primakoff approximation (valid for low excitation regimes) and assumes a second-order truncation of the cumulant expansion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the physical system of spins in a resonator and the simplified waveguide model; Figure 3 illustrates the ROSE protocol, the suppression of ACE by coupling inhomogeneity, and the silencing factor versus coupling width.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of inhomogeneity, develops a new analytical waveguide model, applies this model to explain echo suppression in ROSE/RASE protocols, proposes a mitigation strategy for entanglement, and validates the results against experimental platforms like microwave and optical systems.
why_interesting: It provides a novel perspective on treating disorder (coupling inhomogeneity) as a functional resource rather than a nuisance, which is highly relevant for designing robust quantum networks and high-fidelity quantum memories.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Waveguide model. (a) Physical system: an ensemble of N spins interacts with a single resonator mode, with detunings ωi and couplings gi. The detunings ωi follow a Lorentzian distribution of width Γ (see Appendix G for the non-Lorentzian case), while gi follow an arbitrary distribution (Gaussian with standard deviation σg is used in numerical simulations). (b) Equivalent waveguide model: the ensemble is mapped to a resonator-like bright mode ˆP, and a dark waveguide, formed by a continuum of dark modes ˆPin/out(τ). The mode ˆP couples to ˆa with strength G and to ˆPin/out(τ) with rate Γ. A π-pulse switches the interaction between beam-splitter and two-mode-squeezing forms, while also...

FIG. 2. Effects of adiabatic rapid passage (ARP). (a) For homogeneous coupling, an ARP π-pulse (chirp rate k) ap- plied after an input pulse generates an amplified chirped echo (ACE). (b) Waveguide model picture: ARP inverts different frequency components at different times, producing a chirped wavepacket in the dark waveguide. (c) Silencing by coupling inhomogeneity: in (s, τ) space, Fourier conjugate to (g, ω), the g-dependent phase induced by ARP displaces the state along the s direction, reducing its overlap with the bright mode ˆP and thereby suppressing the ACE.

FIG. 3. ROSE protocol with ARP π-pulses. (a) Schematic: the first π-pulse generates an unwanted ACE, and the second π-pulseretrieves the stored quantum state. (b) Numerical sim- ulation: amplitudes of ⟨ˆain⟩(“Input”) and ⟨ˆaout⟩(“ACE,” “Output”). Solid (dashed) lines show real (imaginary) parts, with input peak normalized to 1. Light green: σg = 0 Hz; dark green: σg = 5 Hz. (c) Silencing factor F vs coupling in- homogeneity σg for different chirp rates k. Markers: numerics; lines: theory [Eq. (5)]. (d) Output noise ⟨∆ˆx2⟩+ ⟨∆ˆp2⟩vs cooperativity C for different σg at fixed k/(2π) = 0.50 MHz2. Markers: numerics; lines: theory [Eq. (8)].

FIG. 4. RASE protocol with ARP π-pulses. (a) Schematic: the first π-pulse generates ASE, entangled with the RASE re- trieved after the second π-pulse. (b) Composite three-π-pulse protocol restoring the entanglement: the second π-pulse is re- placed by three ARP pulses to cancel the coupling-dependent phase. (c) Time correlation ⟨ˆaA(t)ˆaR(t′)⟩plotted as a func- tion of the time offset td from the point of maximum correla- tion, with t′ = 2t0 −t + td; see Appendix D for the definition. FIG. 5. Description of the ROSE protocol in the waveguide model. (a) Ideal π-pulses with an additional echo-silencing mech- anism. (1),(2) Part of the input pulse is absorbed into the dark waveguide, with an efficiency determined by the cooperativity [Eq. (C1)]. (3) A π-pulse reverses the propagation direction of the excitation in the dark waveguide. (4) When the excitation returns to the bright mode ˆP, the excited ensemble produces a noisy echo through the two-mode-squeezing interaction. This echo must be silenced by an external mechanism, such as phase mismatching or cavity detuning. (5) A second π-pulse reverses the propagation direction again.
